text: The patient is positioned in a complete lateral position on the operating table, preparing for the adrenalectomy procedure.
A camera port is inserted at the umbilicus, providing a view for the laparoscopic adrenalectomy procedure.
Two robotic 8mm ports are placed, one at the midclavicular line and another at the anterior axillary line, visible on the patient's lateral side.
A five millimeter port is placed for the assistant, complementing the existing robotic ports and camera port setup for the adrenalectomy procedure.
A twelve millimeter port is placed to accommodate the liver retractor, following the placement of other ports for robotic instruments and camera access.
The surgeon uses a liver retractor to mobilize the liver cephalically, lifting it upwards to expose the surgical field.
Using laparoscopic intraoperative ultrasound, the surgeon guides the dissection of the adrenal vein, which is located near the inferior vena cava (IVC) and adrenal gland.
This provides an accurate, reliable guide for robotic dissection.
The inferior vena cava (IVC) is visible in the surgical field, with robotic instruments docked for the procedure.
The surgeon incises the retroperitoneal covering adjacent to the inferior vena cava (IVC), exposing underlying structures for further dissection.
The surgeon uses camera magnification to precisely dissect the limited space between the adrenal gland and the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgical team proceeds with the adrenalectomy without directly retracting the inferior vena cava (IVC), maintaining a clear view of the anatomical structures.
The accessory adrenal vein is visualized and dissected using a harmonic scalpel, with precise manipulation visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to control the superior pedicle originating from the diaphragmatic branch, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the inferior pedicle originating from the superior polar branch of the renal artery, with the surrounding anatomical structures visible.
The surgeon dissects and divides the inferior lateral attachments of the adrenal gland, using precise techniques facilitated by camera magnification.
The surgeon selectively dissects the accessory adrenal veins using an ultrasonic shear, achieving excellent hemostasis, as evidenced by the absence of bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the final dissection of the adrenal vein at its junction with the inferior vena cava (IVC), using precise techniques to separate the vein from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic shear to safely divide the adrenal vein at its junction with the inferior vena cava, ensuring precise separation and hemostasis.
The surgeon places the adrenal gland specimen into a catch bag following its dissection and removal.
The total console time was eighteen minutes.
The total operative time was forty-nine minutes.
The patient was discharged the next day.
The surgery was carried out without the use of clips or sutures.
The use of the ultrasonic shears for the sealing of vessels during robotic assisted adrenalectomy is safe and effective.
Based on our initial experience, newer energy devices such as the ultrasonic shears are useful tools that facilitate safe adrenal surgery.
